summary: O'Neil examines two recent news events—Deflategate and an overhyped blizzard forecast—to illustrate the public's poor understanding of scientific uncertainty. She argues that both cases involve error bars and measurement variability that the media and public struggle to accept. The NFL hired physicists unnecessarily when simple experiments could demonstrate expected measurement ranges. Weather models correctly predicted storm intensity but erred on location, yet the public blamed the science rather than understanding inherent uncertainty. O'Neil warns this inability to accept imperfect information bodes poorly as society becomes increasingly datafied.
key_points: Both Deflategate and the blizzard forecast controversy stem from misunderstanding error bars and measurement uncertainty
Weather models have improved significantly at predicting storm power, but location and timing remain harder to forecast accurately
The public resists scientific explanations of uncertainty because it challenges our sense of control and omnipotence
Society's assumption that more data equals perfect information is fundamentally flawed
money_quotes: A few good experiments with temperature and friction and lots of measurements by lots of different pressure gauges will empirically demonstrate how much of a range we might expect from such things.
Again, it's a case of errorbars, and the public seems not to understand it. Or maybe they just don't want to.
The truth is, it's not as interesting a story to think about it just happening outside our control.
It messes with our sense of omnipotence and control.
This is bad news for society, as more and more things become 'datafied' and as we assume that will translate into perfect information.
key_insight: Society's growing reliance on data is undermined by a fundamental unwillingness to accept that more data doesn't eliminate uncertainty—it just quantifies it.
